Title: SCIB names CEO Michael Tan as executive director
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Sarawak Consolidated Industries Bhd (SCIB) has appointed its chief executive officer Michael Tan Thian Zhi as executive director.
In a statement, SCIB said the appointment follows Tan's elevation to chief executive officer on May 29.
It also comes as the company continues its transformation into a construction- and engineering, procurement, construction and commissioning (EPCC)-focused company.
The company said Tan brings a combination of engineering expertise, project execution experience and strategic management skills.
He holds a Master of Science in Management from Imperial College London and a Master of Engineering in Civil Engineering from University College London.
Tan began his career as a geotechnical engineer with Borneo Highway PDP Sdn Bhd, the project delivery partner for the Pan Borneo Highway Sabah, before taking on leadership roles in the construction industry.
SCIB chairman Datuk Chong Loong Men said it is entering a new phase where project execution, tender competitiveness and operational discipline will be critical.
"With Tan's experience in infrastructure delivery and contractor-side management, we are confident that he will contribute meaningfully to our efforts to expand our order book and tender book, particularly in Sabah and Sarawak where infrastructure development remains active," he said.
As of May 31 this year, SCIB's outstanding order book amounted to around RM130.93 million across Sabah, Sarawak and West Malaysia, while its tender book stood at RM1.14 billion.
The company recently secured a RM32.78 million EPCC contract to build SMK Balaban Jaya in Beluran, Sabah, comprising 18 classrooms and related facilities.
Tan said he looks forward to working with the board and management to strengthen project execution, expand tender participation and support SCIB's long-term growth.
SCIB said it will remain focused on disciplined project selection, cost management and expanding its construction and EPCC business, with particular emphasis on infrastructure opportunities in Sabah and Sarawak.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: 200 join Umno ahead of Johor election, says candidate
Article:
PONTIAN: About 200 members from several political parties, led by a former Bersatu leader, have joined Umno, citing confidence in the party and Barisan Nasional's leadership ahead of the Johor election.
Barisan Nasional's Pulai Sebatang candidate Hasrunizah Hassan said the defections during the campaign period would provide a boost to the coalition ahead of polling on July 11.
The group was led by former Pontian Bersatu division vice-chief Hairul Azwani A. Karim.
Hairul said the defectors included former members of Bersatu, Pas, PKR and Amanah from Sungai Terus and Kayu Ara Pasong.
"This decision was not made in haste. It was made after considering the views of the grassroots, who want to see closer cooperation for the benefit of the community," he said after the membership handover ceremony at Kampung Parit Baru, Kayu Ara Pasong, last night.
He said all 200 defectors would be registered as members of the Kampung Parit Baru Umno branch, with him serving as the branch's pro-tem chief.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Tyre on NSE kills policeman, injures three
Article:
BUTTERWORTH: A 40-year-old policeman was killed after his motorcycle struck a tyre on the North-South Expressway near Seberang Jaya early today.
Penang police chief Datuk Dennis Lim Kwang Keng said police received a report on the fatal crash at Km140.6 of the North-South Expressway northbound about 6am.
He said initial investigations found that a car travelling from Juru towards Seberang Jaya is believed to have hit a tyre lying in the middle of the road, which was suspected to have fallen from an unidentified lorry.
"The impact caused the driver to lose control before the vehicle veered onto the left road shoulder.
"A motorcycle travelling in the same direction later struck the tyre, causing its rider to fall and be dragged along the road surface.
The victim, a 40-year-old policeman attached to the Perlis contingent police headquarters, was pronounced dead at the scene after suffering severe head injuries.
"The car driver and two passengers sustained injuries and were taken to Seberang Jaya Hospital for further treatment," he said in a statement today.
Lim said a post-mortem conducted at the hospital's Forensic Unit confirmed the cause of death as head injury due to a road traffic crash.
The case is being investigated under Section 41(1) of the Road Transport Act 1987.
Lim said police are tracking down the owner of the tyre, believed to have caused the accident.
He urged anyone with information or dashboard camera footage of the incident to contact investigating officer Inspector Nor Aliza Fatim Ismail at 017-4046215 or the nearest police station.
Lim also reminded motorists to ensure their vehicle components and loads are properly secured before travelling to prevent objects from falling onto roads and endangering other road users.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Over 100 police, family members pay last respects to cop killed in crash
Article:
SEBERANG JAYA: More than 100 police officers, friends and family members gathered at the Seberang Jaya Hospital Forensic Unit today to pay their respects to a policeman who was killed in a road crash.
A sombre atmosphere filled the unit, with many visibly emotional over the loss.
The body arrived at the forensic unit about 8.30am.
Family members were seen embracing one another while colleagues offered comforting words and quiet prayers in an effort to ease their grief.
Uniformed officers stood by the bereaved family throughout the process, gently placing reassuring hands on their shoulders and offering hugs as they mourned the loss of a colleague and friend.
Among those present was Perlis police chief Datuk Muhammad Abdul Halim, who had come to pay his respects.
After the post-mortem was completed, the body left the forensic unit at 12.36pm and was taken back to Perlis for funeral arrangements.
Earlier, the 40-year-old policeman, who was attached to the Perlis contingent police headquarters, was killed after his motorcycle struck a tyre lying on the North-South Expressway near Seberang Jaya about 6am.
Penang police chief Datuk Dennis Lim Kwang Keng said initial investigations found that a car travelling from Juru towards Seberang Jaya had struck a tyre, which was believed to have fallen from an unidentified lorry.
"The impact caused the driver to lose control before the vehicle veered onto the left road shoulder.
"A motorcycle travelling in the same direction later struck the tyre, causing its rider to fall and be dragged along the road surface. The victim was pronounced dead at the scene after suffering severe head injuries.
"The car driver and two passengers sustained injuries and were taken to Seberang Jaya Hospital for further treatment," he said in a statement.
A post-mortem conducted at the hospital's Forensic Unit confirmed the cause of death as head injuries due to a road traffic crash.
The case is being investigated under Section 41(1) of the Road Transport Act 1987.
Police are tracking down the owner of the tyre believed to have caused the accident.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: MARKET PULSE PM JULY 1, 2026 [WATCH]
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Bursa Malaysia ended lower, giving up earlier gains as late-session selling pressure dragged the benchmark index to its intraday low.
The index had traded in positive territory for most of the day before profit-taking accelerated in the final minutes.
This reflects a cautious tone among investors amid a lack of fresh market catalysts.
Ringgit also weakened against the US dollar to 4.0920.
In the crypto market, Bitcoin rebounded to RM242,272.
Ethereum rose to RM6,482 and Solana climbed to RM307.
That wraps up today's Market Pulse.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Sultan Selangor credits Najib, thanks Anwar over LRT3
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Sultan Sharafuddin Idris Shah is pleased with the Shah Alam LRT3 line.
The Selangor sultan expressed his gratitude to and congratulated Prime Minister Datuk Seri Anwar Ibrahim, and gave credit to former prime minister Datuk Seri Najib Razak.
He said he had previously informed Najib of complaints from housewives whose husbands frequently arrived home late due to heavy traffic congestion during their daily commutes.
He said that Najib readily accepted the suggestion to address the issue.
"First of all, I wish to accord recognition to the former prime minister... for acceding to my request that a rail transit facility be built to connect Klang, Shah Alam, and Kuala Lumpur.
"I still recall conveying this recommendation to (Najib) during a breaking of fast event at his residence in 2015, and he accepted my proposal without any hesitation," the ruler said in a statement on his official Facebook page.
Sultan Sharafuddin added that at the time, Klang had only two bridges crossing the Klang River, which severely exacerbated traffic congestion during peak hours.
He also credited Najib for the prompt abolition of the Batu Tiga and Sungai Rasau toll plazas in 2018 as an immediate measure to alleviate the gridlock.
On Anwar, Sultan Sharafuddin acknowledged the role the prime minister had played in ensuring the continuation and completion of the Shah Alam LRT3 project.
The ruler also commended Anwar for his commitment to developing affordable housing near the transit line and reinstating five of the six stations previously cancelled in 2018 during Tun Dr Mahathir Mohamad's second tenure as head of government.
"When (Anwar) assumed the offices of prime minister and finance minister, he agreed to proceed with this project by reinstating five of the cancelled stations.
"He will also develop affordable housing in the vicinity of the stations and along the LRT3 route to enhance convenience for my subjects.
"Furthermore, he has instructed that no further obstructions from any party regarding this project should be entertained.
"I convey my gratitude and congratulations to the (Anwar)," he said.
[[nid:1477880]]
Sultan Sharafuddin remarked that the then-finance minister Lim Guan Eng and his adviser Tony Pua had scaled down the project's costs in 2018 because they did not understand the underlying purpose of the initiative.
"(They did this) on the pretext that it was a 'mega' project. However, they failed to understand that the primary purpose of the LRT3 project was to meet the needs and requirements of the people.
"I wish to emphasise that this is neither a mega project nor an endeavour for self-aggrandisement; rather, it is a project designed for the interests and well-being of my subjects," the ruler stated.
His Royal Highness expressed hope that the
LRT3 would help reduce traffic congestion
and provide a faster, safer, and more comfortable commute for residents of Klang and Shah Alam who work in Kuala Lumpur.
He also envisioned the transit line serving as a catalyst for economic growth, improving the quality of life, and strengthening connectivity among Kuala Lumpur, Petaling Jaya, Shah Alam, and Klang.
"I do not wish to see any party seize the opportunity to claim that the successful execution of the LRT3 project is solely the result of their own efforts.
"In truth, this project is the outcome of continuous planning, commitment, and cooperation among various parties across several administrations, for the benefit of the people of Selangor as a whole," the Sultan of Selangor added.
Meanwhile, in an immediate response, Najib's media team posted on his Facebook page that it was the sultan himself who deserved the primary appreciation for his foresight.
"All credit and appreciation are rightly deserved by His Royal Highness the Sultan of Selangor for his early concern regarding the grievances of the residents of Klang, Shah Alam, and the surrounding areas."
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Ringgit ends lower as Fed rate hike concerns lift US dollar
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: The ringgit ended lower against the US dollar today as expectations of a United States (US) Federal Reserve (Fed) rate hike continued to weigh on regional market sentiment.
At 6pm, the local note depreciated to 4.0920/0965 versus the greenback from 4.0825/0870 at Tuesday's close.
Bank Muamalat Malaysia Bhd chief economist Dr Mohd Afzanizam Abdul Rashid said Asian currencies were generally weaker against the US dollar as investors remained focused on the possibility of further monetary tightening by the Fed.
"Last night, the US Job Openings and Labor Turnover Survey (JOLTS) report showed that the number of job openings was higher than consensus estimates in May.
"Tonight, markets will be observing the ADP Employment Change for June," he told Bernama.
He said the Fed rate hike expectation had continued to support the strength of the US dollar, despite lower crude oil prices.
At the close, the ringgit was mostly lower against a basket of major currencies.
It fell versus the British pound to 5.4203/4262 from 5.4036/4096 at the close on Tuesday, and slid against the euro to 4.6600/6651 from 4.6528/6580 yesterday, but rose against the Japanese yen to 2.5144/5174 from 2.5154/5183 previously.
At the same time, the local note was mixed against regional currencies.
It rose against the Thai baht to 12.2526/2708 from 12.2874/3084 yesterday, and was marginally higher against the Philippine peso at 6.64/6.65 from 6.65/6.67 previously.
It was slightly lower against the Singapore dollar at 3.1555/1589 from 3.1532/1570 at Tuesday's close, and was flat against the Indonesian rupiah at 227.9/228.3 from 227.9/228.3 previously.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Marina claims DAP practised double standards on Najib
Article:
JOHOR BARU: Former Skudai assemblywoman Marina Ibrahim has alleged that a DAP leader supported the idea of granting former prime minister Datuk Seri Najib Razak a royal pardon and allowing him to serve his sentence under house arrest.
In a Facebook post, Marina said she was tired of hearing the same political rhetoric repeated whenever elections approached, particularly over Najib.
She claimed that during a conversation on April 12 with a DAP leader who had previously been a vocal critic of Najib.
"The leader told me that it was not wrong for Najib to be granted a pardon and house arrest due to his past contributions."
Marina alleged that the leader had hoped any pardon would only be granted after the general election to avoid undermining public confidence, particularly among the Chinese community.
She said the conversation shattered her faith in politics and dismissed suggestions that she left because she had been moved to another constituency or was acting out of dissatisfaction.
Marina, who announced in May that
she was quitting politics
and would not defend her Skudai seat, also alleged that DAP had planned to manage public perception through a special party congress originally scheduled for July 12 but later postponed to August.
She claimed the move was intended to regain the confidence of the Chinese community and avoid a repeat of the party's past political setbacks in Sabah.
According to Marina, some party leaders were prepared to resign if Najib received an early pardon while continuing to support the federal government.
She described this as political theatre intended to project principles without significantly affecting the party so close to an election.
Marina questioned whether the renewed political attacks over the issue surfaced only after Johor Umno announced it would not cooperate with DAP ahead of the Johor election.
She noted that Umno's position on Najib had been clear for four years and questioned why DAP had continued seeking cooperation with Barisan Nasional if the matter was truly a non-negotiable red line.
She added that Johor had never had a unity government and argued that DAP should have ended its cooperation much earlier if its principles were absolute.
Marina also claimed DAP leaders were still attempting to secure cooperation with Barisan Nasional ahead of the Johor election.
She said she later discussed the matter with her team and questioned whether manipulating public trust had become an accepted political strategy.
Describing politics as merely a platform, Marina said her relationship with the people she served was akin to family, and she struggled with how she could face constituents who had placed their trust in her.
She said that when she met party leaders again on May 17, she informed them that she needed to consult her team before making any decision.
Defending her team against criticism that it had given her poor advice, Marina said its members included night market traders, painters, single mothers and individuals battling anxiety who had volunteered to serve the community.
Before deciding to leave politics, Marina said she met another Malay political leader on May 20 in the hope of gaining a different perspective.
Instead, she claimed the discussion centred on party positions, the distribution of power and appointments to government-linked companies.
She alleged that the leader described himself as a "flower pot" within the party, expressing frustration over what he perceived as pessimism towards leaders of certain races and his exclusion from key decisions despite holding a senior position.
In her official letter declining the party's offer to contest again, Marina wrote that political leaders should never forget why the people had placed their trust in them.
She also questioned what she described as double standards, saying political principles appeared to change depending on who was involved.
Marina also referred to Umno Youth chief Datuk Dr Muhamad Akmal Saleh, saying he was no newcomer to Barisan Nasional, Umno or the Melaka government.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: VPCM refreshes Golf GTI with new rims
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Volkswagen Passenger Cars Malaysia (VPCM) has unveiled a refreshed look for its hot hatchback, featuring exclusive Huntsville Adamantium Dark rims as a complimentary upgrade for just 50 units of the Golf GTI.
The special offering marks the iconic hot hatch's 50th anniversary celebration.
The upgrade comes at no additional cost to the new Golf GTI's recommended retail price of RM255,990 but with terms and conditions applied.
Featuring a striking dark finish, the 18" 5-spoke alloy wheel with unique Y-spoke design allows a clear view of the GTI's red brake callipers, enhancing the GTI's unmistakable sporty character.
"A fitting tribute to five decades of heritage, driving passion and performance, Malaysian GTI enthusiasts will now have an even more distinctive way to own a piece of GTI history.
"For five decades, the Golf GTI has been recognised around the world as the original hot hatch, an icon that brought performance, practicality and everyday driving excitement together in a way few cars have been able to match," it said.
VPCM said the GTI 50 campaign is the brand's celebration of five decades of GTI heritage, marking the anniversary of its first release in 1976.
"In Malaysia, enthusiasts can look forward to all things GTI this year – from social media giveaways to fun GTI activations at Volkswagen dealerships nationwide," VPCM said.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Unchecked AI progress may pose catastrophic risks, UN panel warns
Article:
GENEVA: Developments in artificial intelligence are outpacing scientific understanding and government policy, meaning there are no guarantees the technology will not cause catastrophic harm, a United Nations independent panel warned on Wednesday.
A preliminary report by the UN's Independent International Scientific Panel on Artificial Intelligence said policymakers face a growing dilemma: they need robust evidence to regulate AI effectively, yet such evidence is struggling to keep pace with the technology's rapid evolution.
"AI capabilities are outpacing both scientific understanding and governments' ability to adapt,"  said Yoshua Bengio, co-chair of the panel, comprised of 40 cross-regional experts.
"With growing evidence of deceptive AI behaviour, science currently cannot guarantee that as capabilities continue to increase, AI will not cause catastrophic harm, either on its own or due to malicious users."
Described as the first global independent assessment of AI's risks and opportunities, the report aims to give up-to-date evaluations of the science to help guide decision-making as governments contend with fast-evolving systems.
In the near term, it expects a shift towards agentic AI systems capable of carrying out real-world tasks, although growth may be constrained by energy and high-quality data shortages.
Over time, it foresees self-improving AI embedded more deeply in the economy and converging with technologies such as quantum computing and biotechnology.
AI already demonstrates expert-level reasoning in mathematics and science and is accelerating drug and vaccine development, and its task complexity is doubling every four to seven months, potentially allowing systems to complete work that takes humans days or weeks, according to the report.
While this could deliver significant economic benefits, it remains unclear whether productivity gains from using AI will translate into broader growth or affect jobs.
The panel also outlined a range of safety concerns, such as the risk of losing control over AI systems as they become increasingly autonomous, and deceptive.
AI is already being used to generate misinformation and other harmful content and could be exploited for fraud, cyberattacks and biological threats.
Governance remains fragmented, with many countries lacking the capacity to assess or shape advanced AI systems, leaving them reliant on technologies they cannot fully understand or control.
Existing safety tools often depend on limited testing data disclosed by companies, the report said.
UN Secretary-General Antonio Guterres urged governments to act swiftly.
"The world cannot govern what it cannot understand," Guterres said in a statement.
"The potential is great, but the risks are real, and the cost of waiting is rising," he added.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Vietnam unveils 'baby bonus' after scrapping two-child policy
Article:
HANOI: One year after lifting its long-standing two-child limit, Vietnam is offering incentives for people to have more babies as the communist country risks getting old before it gets rich.
A new population law and regulations coming into effect Wednesday extend maternity leave from six to seven months for mothers having a second child as well as offering financial help.
If Hanoi residents Nguyen Kim Bich and her husband have a second child, she will get an extra month of maternity leave, free prenatal screenings and a small cash bonus.
"I could stay at home one more month with the baby, and my husband could stay home some more days," the 32-year-old said as her young son romped in a colourful pit of plastic balls.
The new regime subsidises prenatal and newborn screenings and establishes one-off cash bonuses of up to US$228 – two-thirds of the monthly average salary – for mothers who meet certain criteria.
"This is a significant shift in approach," said Pham Thi Lan, head of population and development at the UN Population Fund (UNFPA) in Vietnam.
"We are moving from controlling family planning to focusing on population development."
The change, in a country where communist party members faced sanctions for having a third child until last year, comes as the demographic picture darkens.
Soaring life expectancy and declining birth rates have turned Vietnam into one of the fastest-ageing countries in the world.
These trends reflect the development successes of recent years, but economists warn they could lead to labour shortages and strain the social safety net.
The new population law aims to slow the demographic shift.
But for Bich and her husband Lai, an accountant and advertising professional, the inducements are not enough.
Nearly half their combined US$1,000 monthly income already goes to raising their first child, and they share a small house with his parents.
"The benefits are nice but not enough. One more month of leave and US$75 can never attract us to have a second kid," she said, citing how much of the bonus she would expect to qualify for.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: CeX Malaysia taps Adyen for unified payments
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Global financial technology company Adyen has partnered with consumer electronics retailer CeX Malaysia to streamline payment operations across the retailer's physical stores and online platform.
Since July 2025, CeX Malaysia has been rolling out Adyen's Unified Commerce solution across its e-commerce website and all 11 stores nationwide, enabling the retailer to consolidate payment management and reporting under a single platform.
The move addresses operational challenges arising from CeX Malaysia's hybrid business model, which comprises both corporate-owned and franchise-operated outlets.
Previously, franchisees managed separate payment arrangements with different financial institutions, resulting in fragmented reporting processes and manual reconciliation work.
By adopting Adyen's platform, CeX Malaysia is now able to standardise payment reporting across its network, reducing operational complexity and minimising the risk of human error.
CeX Malaysia head of operations Dan Brannigan said the retailer required a payment partner capable of supporting its growing operations across multiple channels and locations.
He said the partnership has helped simplify reporting processes, reduce manual workload and improve visibility over transactions across the business.
A key component of the collaboration was Adyen's integration with CeX's proprietary point-of-sale software.
The integration is particularly important given CeX's business model, which involves buying, selling and trading electronic products with customers, requiring specialised payment and back-end systems.
Through the platform, CeX Malaysia can settle transactions across multiple payment methods, including DuitNow, GrabPay, Mastercard, Visa and JCB, using a standardised reporting format.
Adyen said the collaboration will also support CeX Malaysia's future growth by laying the groundwork for more connected commerce experiences and deeper customer insights across its retail network.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Unity Merdeka festival set to draw over one million visitors
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: More than one million visitors are expected at Dataran Merdeka for the inaugural Unity Merdeka community festival, which will be held over four days from July 17 to 20.
Held under the theme "United in Diversity, Sustaining Prosperity", the festival is organised by the Kuala Lumpur Police in collaboration with 16 ministries to foster unity among Malaysia's multi-ethnic communities.
Kuala Lumpur police chief Datuk Fadil Marsus said the event will feature activities focusing on community welfare, unity, arts and culture, education, religion, family and entertainment, and is open to people of all ages.
"The event aims to strengthen community ties by promoting unity among Malaysia's diverse ethnic groups, races and cultures through interactive activities, performances by local artistes, and the participation of government agencies and private organisations," he said at the festival's pre-launch ceremony here today.
Among the main attractions are exhibitions and interactive booths by various government agencies, including the police (PDRM), the Malaysian Armed Forces (ATM), the Fire and Rescue Department of Malaysia (JBPM) and the Health Ministry (MOH).
Participating organisations also include Technical and Vocational Education and Training (TVET) institutions, Universiti Tun Abdul Razak (UNITAR), Pos Malaysia, the National Registration Department (JPN) and the National Anti-Drugs Agency (AADK), he said.
Other highlights include the Police Cadet Marching Competition, the Community Care corporate social responsibility (CSR) programme, a meet-and-greet session with animation characters, children's activities, e-sports competitions, pickleball, Muay Thai matches, a five-kilometre Fun Run and a Fun Bike Ride.
The event will also feature the 'KL Berselawat' programme and a live screening of the 2026 FIFA World Cup final on a giant screen.
Meanwhile, Fadil advised motorists to plan their journeys and cooperate with the authorities to help ensure smooth traffic flow around the capital throughout the four-day event.
Members of the public seeking further information may contact the Kuala Lumpur Police Hotline at 03-2115 9999, the Tun H.S. Lee Traffic Police Station at 03-2071 9999, or their nearest police station. - BERNAMA
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Kelantan police hunt 'Mejoh' in Thailand over drug-debt abduction of two men
Article:
KOTA BARU: Kelantan police have sought the assistance of their Thai counterparts to track down the main suspect believed to be behind the abduction of two men in Tumpat before the victims were found in Thailand on June 8.
Kelantan police chief Datuk Mohd Yusoff Mamat said the 40-year-old suspect, known as "Mejoh", is believed to be hiding in Thailand.
"We have also received information that the suspect has a wife there.
"We have sought assistance from the Thai police to locate the main suspect," he said after receiving a courtesy call from Media Prima Berhad chairman Datuk Seri Dr Syed Hussian Aljunid at his office here today.
The two men were abducted in Tumpat and Pasir Mas on June 6 over an alleged RM33,000 debt linked to drug-related activities and the smuggling of undocumented migrants.
They were found in Mundok, Thailand, two days later.
One of the victims, a 51-year-old man with 24 criminal records, was allegedly abducted together with another man as "collateral" for the actual borrower, who was their relative.
Following the incident, police launched a manhunt for two main suspects, identified as Mohamad Syamsul Azroy Che Anuar, 32, and Hamzah Muhamad, 40.
One of the suspects was arrested in Terengganu on June 21.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Cybercrimes Bill must protect journalists, whistleblowers acting in public interest, says MP
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: The Cybercrimes Bill should include explicit safeguards for journalists, whistleblowers and cybersecurity researchers acting responsibly to ensure the law targets criminals without undermining legitimate public interest activities.
Datuk Ahmad Marzuk Shaary (PN-Pengkalan Chepa) said the proposed legislation must clearly protect responsible journalism, whistleblowing, cybersecurity research and legally privileged communications, including those between lawyers and their clients.
"The bill must provide clear protection for journalists, whistleblowers, cybersecurity researchers acting responsibly, as well as communications protected under the law, including those between lawyers and their clients.
"The key issue is ensuring the law pursues criminals rather than creating fear among ordinary Malaysians who wish to speak out," he told the Dewan Rakyat when debating the bill.
He said public concern was no longer about whether Malaysia lacked sufficient laws, but whether existing laws were being enforced fairly and consistently.
"People are asking whether they will still be able to criticise the government after this. The government cannot take such concerns lightly.
"What the people want is not merely a tough law, but one that is fair, balanced and trusted not to be abused."
Marzuk said concerns stemmed from perceptions surrounding the enforcement of existing laws, particularly Section 233 of the Communications and Multimedia Act, which critics claimed had at times been used against individuals criticising the government and authorities.
He said public confidence in the administration of justice was as important as justice itself.
"The principle that justice must not only be done, but must also be seen to be done, is crucial.
"Once public confidence in the fair enforcement of the law begins to erode, confidence in the justice system will also decline."
He also cited concerns raised by the Malaysian Media Council, which had proposed that the bill be scrutinised by a parliamentary special select committee, as well as views expressed by Article 19, the Centre for Independent Journalism and Sinar Project on issues involving investigative powers, privacy, media freedom and freedom of expression.
Marzuk said experiences under existing laws had also contributed to fears of a "chilling effect", where individuals refrained from speaking out because they were worried about investigations rather than prosecution.
He cited cases where journalists had their communication devices seized during investigations, saying such actions could compromise confidential sources and discourage future whistleblowers from exposing wrongdoing.
He also proposed that powers to access data, search premises, seize digital devices and obtain communications should be subject to strict judicial oversight.
"The government must also table annual reports to Parliament detailing the use of powers under the proposed law to strengthen parliamentary oversight."
He also urged the government to explicitly state that legitimate political criticism, ethical journalism, public interest disclosures and responsible cybersecurity research should not be grounds for exercising the bill's extraordinary enforcement powers.
"If we succeed in pursuing criminals without frightening the public, that will be the true success of this bill," he said.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Bursa Malaysia reverses earlier gains to end lower
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Bursa Malaysia's benchmark index fell 0.43 per cent to close at its intraday low, reversing earlier gains as a wave of late-session selling erased the day's advances.
IPPFA Sdn Bhd director of investment strategy and country economist Mohd Sedek Jantan said the index traded in positive territory for much of the day before profit-taking intensified in the final minutes, reflecting investors' cautious stance amid a lack of fresh market catalysts.
At 5pm, the FTSE Bursa Malaysia KLCI (FBM KLCI) eased 7.23 points to 1,656.83 from Tuesday's close of 1,664.06.
The benchmark index opened 1.29 points higher at 1,665.35 and climbed to an intraday high of 1,670.99 in the mid-morning session, before losing momentum in the final 10 minutes, dragging the index to its session low at the close.
Market breadth was positive, with gainers outnumbering decliners 526 to 448, while 559 counters were unchanged, 1,088 untraded, and 14 suspended.
Turnover decreased to 2.51 billion units valued at RM2.26 billion from 2.91 billion units valued at RM2.77 billion yesterday.
Mohd Sedek said that despite the weaker close, yesterday's RM199 million net buying by local institutional investors is expected to continue providing an important buffer against persistent foreign selling pressure.
However, he told Bernama that near-term market sentiment is likely to remain fragile until stronger domestic catalysts emerge or there is greater clarity on the global interest rate outlook.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Johor leads states with 8pct growth as Malaysia's economy expands 5.2pct
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Malaysia's economy expanded by 5.2 per cent in 2025, with gross domestic product (GDP) reaching RM1.74 trillion, according to the Department of Statistics Malaysia (DOSM).
Johor recorded the highest growth among the states at 8 per cent, with its economy valued at RM171 billion, accounting for nearly 10 per cent of the national economy. The growth was driven largely by the rapid expansion of its data centre industry and digital economy infrastructure.
Chief Statistician Datuk Seri Dr Mohd Uzir Mahidin said three other states also outperformed the national growth rate: Penang (7.3 per cent), Selangor (6.3 per cent) and Perak (5.7 per cent).
Together, Selangor, Kuala Lumpur, Johor, Sarawak and Penang remained the country's main economic engines, contributing a combined 67.9 per cent of Malaysia's GDP.
Selangor retained its position as the largest contributor to the national economy, accounting for 26.5 per cent of GDP with an output of RM460.1 billion.
Six states and federal territories recorded GDP per capita above the national average of RM59,167, led by Kuala Lumpur at RM144,898, followed by Putrajaya (RM126,359), Labuan (RM88,764), Penang (RM80,584), Sarawak (RM73,757) and Selangor (RM70,362).
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: France introduces extra parental leave to boost birth rates
Article:
PARIS: France on Wednesday launched new paid leave for both mothers and fathers, as President Emmanuel Macron seeks to boost the country's sluggish birth rate.
In 2024, Macron unveiled a plan to combat infertility and the declining birth rate, saying it would help pave the way towards what he described as France's "demographic re-armament."
Until now, paid maternity leave in France has been just under four months for a first child, while fathers have been entitled to 28 days of leave following a birth.
Under the new rules, which came into force on Wednesday, mothers and fathers can now share an additional leave of one or two months on top of existing entitlements.
Parents will receive 70 per cent of their net salary during the first month and 60 per cent during the second.
The new provisions apply to parents of children born on or after Jan 1, 2026. Adoptive parents are also eligible.
Fertility rates are declining across the European Union.
France last year – for the first time since the end of World War II – recorded fewer babies born than people dying in the country, according to the National Statistics Institute.
Feminist groups say the reform falls short and is unlikely to improve gender equality.
They note that the lower-earning partner – typically the woman – is more likely to take the additional leave.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Kelantan police chief advises Malaysians to delay non-essential trips to southern Thailand
Article:
KOTA BARU: Kelantan police have advised Malaysians, especially those from the state, to postpone non-essential travel to southern Thailand after five shooting and bomb incidents were reported within a week.
Kelantan police chief Datuk Mohd Yusoff Mamat said while authorities cannot stop the public from visiting the area, regular security advice will be issued from time to time.
"I always advise people to avoid visiting southern Thailand because we receive constant updates. Our Special Branch officers continuously report on the situation there," he said.
"Currently, there have been five shooting and bomb incidents within a week. That is why I am asking those without urgent matters or important business to postpone their trips. If you are only going there to eat, there is no need," he said.
Yusoff said this after receiving a courtesy call from Media Prima Bhd chairman Datuk Seri Dr Syed Hussian Aljunid at his office here today.
He added that the final decision to travel ultimately rests with individual travellers.
"If you ask me, reduce your travel there and support domestic tourism instead," he said.
Previously, it was reported that two Malaysians were injured in a bomb blast in Tak Bai, Thailand, at 11.40am on Monday.
The victims, identified as Abdullah Syarapi Abd Rahman, 45, and Muhammad Yusri Udin, 38, were on their way back to Malaysia after a holiday in Narathiwat.
They received treatment at a hospital in Narathiwat before being transferred to Hospital Raja Perempuan Zainab II here yesterday morning for further treatment.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Storms following heatwave kill two in Romania, Austria
Article:
BUCHAREST: Storms that followed an intense European heatwave killed one person in Romania and another in Austria, authorities said on Wednesday.
Storms lashed parts of Romania including the capital Bucharest, during the night from Tuesday to Wednesday, flooding some basements and homes and tearing down trees.
Footage released by the emergency services showed them clearing tree branches from cars and people wading through waist-high water.
In Ganeasa, just outside of Bucharest, a person was found dead inside a car struck by a falling tree, they said.
Storms have also lashed Austria, causing landslides in Tyrol province and elsewhere since Sunday.
A man died on Tuesday after being hit by a falling branch in Perg district in Upper Austria province, police said.
The 26-year-old was driving his quad, together with his partner, when a roadside tree branch – battered by a storm – broke off and hit him, police said.
Storms have followed a severe heatwave that lasted more than a week and broke temperature records across Europe.
Experts say climate change caused by greenhouse gas emissions generated by human activities is increasing the frequency and intensity of extreme weather events.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Budi Diesel rollout eases burden on private diesel users
Article:
SHAH ALAM: Private diesel vehicle users in the Klang Valley say the nationwide implementation of the Budi Diesel subsidy has helped ease operating costs after months of coping with higher fuel prices.
Food vendor Abdul Hakim, 33, who has operated a kiosk in Section 7 here for almost five years, said the subsidy had reduced transportation costs for his catering work and helped him maintain affordable food prices.
"When diesel prices were high, we had to charge customers an additional transportation fee for catering orders. Now, we do not have to burden customers as much.
"I sell student-priced meals, with laksa costing RM6 while many places charge RM7 or RM8. The subsidy helps us maintain those prices," he said.
Hakim said his employer had previously switched to a petrol-powered van after diesel prices surged, as operating a diesel vehicle had become too costly.
"We had to adapt just to survive. Now, with the subsidy, we can finally breathe again."
Lorry driver Muhammad Syahrul Ahmad, 45, who has worked for a bread company for 13 years, said the lower diesel price had reduced weekly fuel expenses for delivery runs around Klang.
"I normally refuel twice a week, spending about RM300 each time. Since the subsidised price came in, there have been noticeable savings.
"I hope the government can maintain the current price. The cheaper it is, the better for everyone," he said.
Meanwhile, Muhammad Azhar Abdul Razak, 39, who drives for a private fruit distribution business, said the subsidy had eased some financial pressure, especially as fuel costs remained a major expense for businesses relying on daily travel.
Based in Subang and operating mainly around Shah Alam, Azhar regularly travels to Johor to collect durians, with diesel costs reaching about RM300 per day for a single trip and close to RM1,000 a week on some routes.
He said this year had been among the toughest in his five years in the business, as weaker consumer purchasing power had affected fruit sales.
"The RM2.10 price is a big relief and we are grateful, but the 200-litre quota is not enough because our business depends on travelling long distances. We hope the government can maintain the subsidy," he said.
For Badrul Amin Abdul Karim, 29, whose family has operated Rojak Karim since 1995, the subsidy is expected to reduce transportation costs for daily operations.
Badrul said the business makes daily trips between its central kitchen in Seri Muda and its stall in Section 9, Shah Alam.
Previously, he spent about RM150 on diesel each week, but with the subsidised price, he expects a full tank to last up to 12 days instead of less than a week, reducing refuelling to about three times a month.
However, he said the family's diesel-powered pickup truck used for catering jobs around Kuala Lumpur remained a major expense before the subsidy, requiring refuelling every two days at nearly RM200 each time without even filling the tank fully.
"Alhamdulillah, we are very grateful because this helps us save on operating costs. We appreciate the government's assistance and hope the subsidy can be maintained, especially for diesel users like us," he said.
Although users said the current subsidy had eased their burden, some said a higher allocation would provide more flexibility, particularly for those covering long distances for business operations.
Hakim said a larger quota would be helpful, but the current allocation was already a significant improvement.
"If the government gives more, of course, it would help even more. But what we have now is already better than having nothing," he said.
The Budi Diesel subsidy scheme took full effect nationwide today, allowing eligible private diesel vehicle users to purchase subsidised diesel at RM2.10 per litre.
The government had earlier announced that the subsidised diesel price would be lowered from RM2.15 to RM2.10 per litre, benefiting about 700,000 private diesel vehicle owners nationwide.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Expected next UK PM Burnham faces defence funding gap
Article:
LONDON, July 1 (Reuters) - Andy Burnham will have to find an additional £4.7 billion (US$6.2 billion) to close a defence funding gap, either through deeper spending cuts or higher taxes, if, as expected, he becomes British prime minister later this month.
Prime Minister Keir Starmer on Tuesday announced a long-delayed defence plan aimed at making Britain's depleted armed forces war-ready amid rising security threats and warnings that Russia could attack a Nato member as soon as 2030.
However, the plan's commitment to spend an additional £15 billion came under scrutiny within hours of its release after accompanying documents showed that a third of the funding still needed to be found in a budget later this year.
"It's not unusual for governments to make announcements saying this is what we'll spend, and then to complete the details of that at the next budget," Luke Pollard, minister of defence procurement, told Sky News on Wednesday.
Burnham is widely expected to become Britain's prime minister later in July. His view of Starmer's defence plan is not yet known.
"I know that if Andy Burnham becomes the prime minister ... that he will take national security as seriously as Keir has taken it," Pollard said.
The plan was also criticised by opposition politicians and former military chiefs for failing to set out when defence spending would reach 3 per cent of GDP, on the way to meeting Britain's Nato commitment to spend 3.5 per cent of GDP by 2035.
Starmer defended the costings on Tuesday, saying much of the additional funding would come from reallocating spending from other government departments.
The plans have also drawn criticism for cutting funding earmarked for road and energy projects, a sensitive issue for a government elected in 2024 on promises of long-term investment in infrastructure.
The Institute for Fiscal Studies, a think tank, said the £1.2 billion per year needed to fund the current plan meant the next prime minister would quickly face difficult choices.
"There will be further impacts on other areas of spending, tax or borrowing on top of those set out in today's announcements – implying one key early decision for the next prime minister," the IFS said.
"Defence spending will likely remain one of the biggest fiscal pressures facing the UK in the medium term," it added, saying that reaching 3.5 per cent in 2035 would require an additional £25 billion a year.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Venezuelans search, suffer one week after deadly quakes
Article:
VENEZUELANS, with help from around the world, were still searching Wednesday for survivors a week after massive earthquakes killed nearly 2,000 and left thousands unaccounted for.
Survivors were mourning while battling shortages of food and shelter, but searches of the scores of flattened apartment buildings have carried on and saved lives.
A three-year-old boy was rescued alive from the rubble in Caracas on Tuesday, a Jordanian rescue team said, a full six days after Venezuela's most powerful quake in over a century.
"We're late, we're very late... but our aim is to keep saving lives, to be able to save those citizens who are trapped under the rubble, who still need us," said Luis Arteaga Benatuil, member of Spanish search and rescue group, after landing arriving Wednesday in Venezuela.
The rescues have come long after the 72-hour period considered critical for survival and as the UN refugee agency said "food shortages are widespread in hard-hit port city of La Guaira.
"The situation is quite critical," said Lia Poggio, head of mission in Venezuela for the International Organisation for Migration (IOM).
The 7.2 and 7.5 magnitude shocks – one of the worst earthquake disasters in Latin American history – collapsed whole residential complexes on June 24 and prompted frantic search-and-rescue operations for survivors trapped in the ruins.
Venezuela's National Assembly President Jorge Rodriguez said Tuesday that deaths had risen to 1,943, with more than 10,500 injured after one of Latin America's worst earthquake disasters.
[[nid:1477783]]
He added that nearly 6,500 people had been rescued from the rubble in La Guaira, but that number was likely closer to 20,000 including those who escaped or were helped out by family.
Many Venezuelans have not hidden their anger at the government's slow response to the disaster in a country already struggling with decades of economic crisis that has weakened infrastructure and health services.
"They give out supplies here, but sometimes people nearly kill each other for food... It's like a cockfight," Daniela Armas, 18, a vendor in La Guaira, said after waiting to get food an emergency shelter.
The UN agency said it needed some US$14.85 million to scale up aid and temporary shelter for 30,000 people over six months.
The quakes likely damaged or destroyed 58,870 buildings, according to a preliminary assessment of satellite data published by NASA.
With thousands in need of help, World Health Organisation spokesman Christian Lindmeier said health services in Venezuela were overstretched and under "extreme pressure."
"There's an increased risk now of outbreaks of vaccine-preventable diseases" such as measles and diphtheria, due to low pre-earthquake vaccination coverage, he said.
As international rescue teams from the US, Mexico and dozens of other countries scrambled with trained dogs and heavy equipment to dig out survivors, Venezuelans began burying the dead they could find.
Others were frantically searching by hand for missing loved ones in the rubble, but also in hospitals and morgues.
[[nid:1477782]]
Darvin Silva, 37, described how he battled to reach his mother, who died under a pillar in a collapsed building.
"The effort it took me to get her out of there with my bare hands, with sledgehammers, with pickaxes... you can't even begin to imagine," he said.
"I hope that I can now offer her the rest she deserves," he said. Around 50,000 people are still listed as missing, according to the UN.
Some seven million people in Venezuela would be affected by the disaster, the UN has said, with the quakes knocking a US$6.7-billion hole in the economy – or six per cent of Venezuela's GDP.
A total of 27 countries have mobilised nearly 40 search-and-rescue teams. They include more than 2,000 troops and personnel, along with more than 160 dogs, according to Gianluca Rampolla, the UN coordinator in Venezuela.
At the only public cemetery in Caracas, the two crematory ovens have been working at full capacity.
At the makeshift morgue at La Guaira's port, many are still waiting for the remains of their loved ones who are presumed dead.
"My family is there – I'm told my sister and her children are there, as well as the children of my brother," Wilker Molalla told AFP as he waited to identify the remains.
"There were 11 people in my household," he said. "Only two of us survived because we were at work."
* The writer is from AFP
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Cybercrimes Bill targets users but lets AI companies off the hook, says MP
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: The Cybercrimes Bill 2026 does not go far enough in addressing the root causes of digital offences as it fails to regulate powerful artificial intelligence (AI) companies that provide the tools enabling them.
Machang member of parliament Wan Ahmad Fayhsal Wan Ahmad Kamal said that while the bill has its merits, it remains insufficient to comprehensively tackle the issue.
"The source of deepfakes continues to operate — the powerful AI companies that help people create deepfakes are still not regulated by law," he said during the debate on the bill in the Dewan Rakyat today.
He also called for stronger dedicated cybersecurity units to be established at every ministry, agreeing with a point raised by Datuk Rosol Wahid (PN-Hulu Terengganu) that the government should not rely solely on the Malaysian Communications and Multimedia Commission (MCMC) to protect its systems.
Rosol had earlier referenced the Health Ministry website, which was
hacked on June 27.
"We need a more established cybersecurity team in all ministries to address these new threats," Rosol said.
"We need a more established cybersecurity team in all ministries to address these new threats," he said.
Wan Ahmad Fayhsal said while he supported the broad thrust of the bill, it effectively targets only end users while leaving the structural power of AI tech firms untouched.
He questioned two specific provisions in the bill: the power to conduct warrantless searches, asking what oversight mechanisms exist to prevent abuse, and the definition of content that "falsely appears to be authentic".
He expressed concern over whether the definition was clear enough to avoid being used to suppress critical voices, including religious scholars and academics debating AI issues openly.
He called on the government to involve stakeholders beyond the legislative process to strengthen ethical frameworks, citing Pope Leo XIV's recent encyclical 'Magnifica Humanitas' and its call for AI to be humanised.
"Without a strong grounding in religion and ethics, this crime will continue to recur and become more complex — because without law and religion, humans will become unruly beasts," he said.
He added that he supported the bill but urged improvements to clauses that risked encroaching on the democratic rights and civil liberties of Malaysians.
Earlier today, Deputy Prime Minister Datuk Seri Dr Ahmad Zahid Hamidi tabled the
Cybercrimes Bill 2026
for its second reading in the Dewan Rakyat, which seeks to repeal the Computer Crimes Act 1997.
Key features of the bill include the expansion of offences related to computer systems and data, including hacking, malware and ransomware attacks, unauthorised interception of communications, computer-related fraud and forgery, as well as crimes involving the national digital identity service, MyDigital ID.
On Monday, the Malaysian Media Council called for the bill to be referred to a parliamentary select committee for further scrutiny.
The council warned that because the bill grants extensive surveillance and interception powers to prevent any crime under any law, it could create a chilling effect on investigative journalism, whistleblowing, and attorney-client confidentiality without clear statutory safeguards.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: RM2.10 diesel among world's cheapest as Malaysia rolls out subsidy reform
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Malaysians continue to enjoy one of the most competitive diesel prices globally, reinforcing the country's social protection framework amid persistent global energy cost pressures.
Market observers said the subsidised diesel price of RM2.10 per litre under the Budi Madani diesel programme remains among the lowest globally, even as international diesel prices stay elevated and volatile.
At the current market rate of RM3.97 per litre (July 1-8), the targeted subsidy mechanism continues to absorb the price gap for eligible users, reinforcing Malaysia's position as a country still offering heavily supported fuel access despite ongoing subsidy rationalisation.
Eligible Malaysians can now buy subsidised diesel at RM2.10 per litre under the diesel programme effective July 1.
The rollout reflects the government's commitment to shielding citizens from cost-of-living pressures while ensuring fuel subsidies are no longer broadly distributed to unintended beneficiaries.
With MyKad used as the verification mechanism, diesel subsidies are now channelled more directly, accurately and in a more controlled manner to eligible Malaysians.
The approach is also aimed at preventing blanket subsidy leakage, including misuse by non-citizens or parties involved in diesel smuggling and other illicit activities.
Analysts viewed the targeted subsidy mechanism as aligned with Malaysia's fiscal consolidation agenda, with research houses broadly describing it as a positive step for the country's financial position.
Maybank Investment Bank Bhd previously described fuel subsidy rationalisation as supportive of fiscal strengthening efforts, with potential government savings of around RM2 billion per year, helping to narrow the fiscal deficit.
CIMB Securities, meanwhile, expects the inflationary impact to be contained and temporary, given the targeted nature of the mechanism which limits broad-based pass-through effects to consumers.
RHB Research noted that cost pressures are likely concentrated in specific sectors such as logistics and transportation, though the impact is expected to be manageable and partially offset by targeted assistance measures.
Kenanga Research views the policy as enhancing Malaysia's medium-term fiscal credibility, while also strengthening investor confidence in the government's commitment to subsidy reform.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: No corn dogs? Trump's 'Great American State Fair' threatens to be a flop
Article:
DESERTED booths, sparse crowds and prayer tents. Welcome to the Great American State Fair, where a celebration of the country's 250th birthday has morphed into a new political battlefront.
Summers in Washington are notoriously steamy, but the proverbial heat has soared over the 16-day fair and how people view President Donald Trump's tight grip on the event.
"This is once in a lifetime," military veteran Rhonda Smith, a 42-year-old living in Virginia, told AFP on Tuesday as she ducked into one pavilion to escape the punishing sun on the National Mall.
"It's important to support our president and the beautification of DC and all the things that go with it," she said, dismissing the naysayers and adding "I think everyone should attend."
Critics have not been so kind.
"Underwhelming," scoffed Jesse, a 50-year-old therapist as he walked on the mall.
"As soon as Trump takes something over, he makes it political, he makes it about himself, and that's why it's so empty," said Jesse, who gave only his first name.
The fair was conceived years ago by a bipartisan group of lawmakers as a celebration of American history, culture and innovation.
But Trump's Freedom 250 group has largely taken over the festivities – triggering a bitter clash over control, and harsh criticism over the lacklustre exhibits and downbeat atmosphere.
Each state has a booth, but some are virtually empty after several states – Massachusetts and North Carolina among them – declined to officially participate.
Pavilion structures have ill-fitting plastic coverings that contrast with the timeless marble and granite Smithsonian museums nearby.
Jesse and others argue the fair lacks the all-American character that is part of state fairs across the country, where youths handle livestock, ride on rollercoasters and gorge on sweet delicacies such as funnel cake.
There are whiffs of genuine Americana on the mall: a large Ferris wheel, a rodeo and multiple stages hosting music and talks.
[[nid:1477764]]
But there are no life-size butter cows, or livestock shows, tractor pulls, or corn dogs – a classic deep-fried snack.
Instead, US government agencies are featured, including the Department of Defence, with a "War" banner hanging outside its pavilion. Inside, children tried on bullet-proof vests.
In another government pavilion, images of a newly re-designed US passport, featuring Trump's likeness, are on display.
Near the centre of the mall stands a small-scale replica of the vast "triumphal arch" that the president wants erected in the capital.
It proved a bit much even for Gary Freyer, a Trump supporter attending from neighbouring Maryland, who wrinkled his nose at the arch and considered how the president has sought to put his stamp on the fair.
"He wants to be remembered," the 76-year-old told AFP. "I think it's a little overboard."
Several exhibitors have also drawn attention, including Hillsdale College, a small school influential with conservatives, the Museum of the Bible, and Revival Ministries International.
"Can I pray for you?" asked a man outside a revival tent to fair attendees walking past.
Online criticism has been ferocious, with influencers deriding the fair as a boondoggle co-opted by the White House.
"Not a funnel cake, corn dog, bucket lemonade, or fried Snickers in sight," local politician Christina Henderson wrote on social media.
"The only ride is the Ferris Wheel. It's not that kind of state fair, y'all."
As she wandered through the booths and pavilions, Spaniard Laura Rodriguez, looked on the positive side.
"I don't really like crowded places," she said.
* The writer is from AFP
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Pas afraid to face DAP in Johor polls, says Loke
Article:
JOHOR BARU: A Pakatan Harapan leader has claimed that Pas has chosen to rely on Barisan Nasional because the Islamist party is afraid to contest head-on against DAP in the Johor election.
DAP secretary-general Anthony Loke said Pas decided to consolidate the votes of its members behind BN since the party is aware of its slim chances, given that the majority of seats in Johor are mixed constituencies.
"If they do not field candidates, their strategy is to consolidate the votes of their members behind BN, specifically MCA, in order to defeat the DAP candidate.
"This is their strategy because they know that if they field candidates in those seats, they will not be able to win, but they will still be able to split a portion of the votes.
"They (Pas) believe those votes will shift to BN," Loke, who is also transport minister, told reporters after attending a ceramah for PH candidate for Penggaram, Felicia Poh Rui Ling, in Batu Pahat last night.
As such, Loke said he hoped more people would turn out to vote on polling day on July 11.
"It is important to make voters aware that this is their strategy.
"If we want to retain PH seats, the most important factor is a high voter turnout. Only then can we defeat our opponents," he said.
In the 2022 state election, PH candidate Gan Peck Cheng won the seat with a majority of 9,956 votes.
She polled 18,208 votes to defeat three other contenders for the seat.
For this state election, Poh is in a straight fight against BN candidate Boo Chin Leong.
Pas recently issued an instruction telling party members and supporters to vote for BN in seats where Perikatan Nasional is not contesting.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Malaysia to have rocket manufacturing plant in two years, missiles to follow
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Malaysia is set to establish a local rocket manufacturing facility within two years under a strategic partnership between Weststar Defence Industries Sdn Bhd and Australia's Rocket Technologies International Pty Ltd (RTI).
Defence Minister Datuk Seri Mohamed Khaled Nordin said the collaboration would eventually expand into missile production, which would strengthen Malaysia's sovereign defence capability and reduce reliance on foreign suppliers.
He said the partnership, signed during his three-day official working visit to Australia, also covers technology transfer, research and development (R&D), human capital development and collaboration with higher education institutions.
"The government welcomes and fully supports this strategic partnership as it is in line with the nation's efforts to develop local capabilities in the development and production of rockets and missiles.
"RTI's expertise in rocket and missile technologies would provide Malaysia with access to advanced capabilities while allowing local industries to participate in high-value defence manufacturing.
"Weststar's investment would also enable Malaysia to become part of RTI's global supply chain, opening opportunities for local companies to expand within the international defence ecosystem," he said in a statement.
The agreement was signed between Weststar Group founder and group managing director Tan Sri Dr Syed Azman Syed Ibrahim and RTI founder Allan James Payne at the National Convention Centre in Canberra, witnessed by Khaled.
Khaled added that the initiative is in line with the National Defence Industry Policy (DIPN), which seeks to strengthen the local defence industry and enhance the country's sovereign defence capability.
He also expressed appreciation to the Australian government for supporting the collaboration and approving the technology transfer between RTI and Weststar.
"The venture would not only generate significant returns for Malaysia's defence industry but also strengthen the nation's long-term defence capabilities for future generations," he said.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Woman who tried to hug Sultan Nazrin must face trial after bid to drop charge fails
Article:
IPOH: The prosecution has rejected a representation by a woman charged with using criminal force against the Sultan of Perak, Sultan Nazrin Muizzuddin Shah during the state-level National Day celebration last year.
Deputy public prosecutor Nasrul Hadi Abdul Ghani told the magistrate's court today that the prosecution had rejected the application seeking the withdrawal of the charge.
"For the case this afternoon, as I informed the management earlier, the instruction from our side is to proceed with the case, as the representation submitted by the defence has been reviewed and examined.
"Among other things, the representation sought the withdrawal of the charge. However, the instruction we received is to proceed with the prosecution," he said.
The case was mentioned before Magistrate Norizan Riduan.
The prosecution was also represented by deputy public prosecutor S. Nishaalini, while the accused was represented by lawyer Zulzarif Zakuan.
The court adjourned the proceedings after lead defence counsel Datuk Naran Singh was unable to attend.
Case management was fixed for July 6.
Nurhaswani Afni Mohamad Zorki, 42, is
charged with using criminal force
after she attempted to hug Sultan Nazrin during the state-level National Day celebration on Aug 31 last year.
She was charged under Section 352 of the Penal Code, which carries a maximum sentence of three months' imprisonment, a fine of up to RM1,000, or both, upon conviction.
On Feb 12 this year, the defence told the court that it had submitted a representation to the Attorney-General's Chambers on Feb 6 seeking to have the charge withdrawn.
On Oct 8 last year, the court rejected the accused's guilty plea after the prosecution informed the court that a report from Hospital Bahagia Ulu Kinta found she was suffering from schizophrenia and was of unsound mind at the time of the incident.
On Sept 8, 2025, she was brought before the same court, but no plea was recorded after she was ordered to
undergo a one-month psychiatric observation
at Hospital Bahagia under Section 342 of the Criminal Procedure Code.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: What everyone gets wrong about the Petronas debate
Article:
LETTERS:
When the Strait of Hormuz closed in late February, Malaysia was expected to face serious difficulties. Most analysts predicted the worst — soaring oil prices, supply disruptions and a regional economic crisis.
Some of that materialised. But Malaysia coped better than expected. Growth forecasts were revised upwards rather than downwards, fuel supplies remained stable and gas export commitments were met.
A key reason was Petronas.
That is why what is happening to the national oil company deserves far greater attention than it is currently receiving.
The question is not whether Petronas is managed perfectly, or whether criticism directed at the company is unfounded. Some concerns are legitimate.
The more important question is whether a country can continue relying on its strongest company from every direction at once — for tax revenue, legal disputes and crisis management — without gradually weakening the very institution that makes it so valuable.
MORE THAN A TYPICAL GLC
Malaysia has many government-linked companies, but most operate in protected domestic markets.
Petronas is different.
When the Petroleum Development Act was introduced in 1974, the objective was not merely to collect oil revenue. It was to build a national energy company capable of competing with the world's leading oil and gas firms by acquiring expertise, developing local engineering talent and operating at an international level.
That ambition succeeded.
Economist Pietro Masina has described Petronas as one of the few companies from a former colony to master the complexities of the global energy industry — from exploration and deepwater drilling to liquefied natural gas exports and large-scale international operations.
Many resource-rich countries generated wealth from oil but failed to develop the technical expertise needed to sustain their industries. Malaysia achieved both.
Today, Petronas operates in more than 100 countries, maintains sales offices in 22 markets and successfully managed the Strait of Hormuz disruption without requiring external assistance.
MORE THAN A DISPUTE OVER REVENUE
Much of the debate surrounding Petronas centres on how oil and gas revenue should be distributed between the federal government, Sabah, Sarawak and the company itself.
These are legitimate questions rooted in history.
Sabah and Sarawak produce a significant share of Malaysia's oil and gas resources, and the dispute over control and revenue sharing is currently before the courts.
Whatever the outcome, prolonged uncertainty complicates investment decisions, particularly in an industry where projects often take decades to deliver returns.
The issue is not deciding who is right or wrong.
Rather, every competing claim — justified or otherwise — ultimately places additional pressure on Petronas, which must continue operating while those disputes remain unresolved.
History offers clear lessons.
National oil companies that encountered long-term decline were not necessarily those subjected to scrutiny over public finances. They were the ones governments repeatedly treated as unlimited sources of revenue.
The pattern is familiar: profits are continually extracted while investment declines, production weakens, debt increases and experienced professionals leave.
The decline rarely happens overnight. It emerges gradually through underinvestment and the steady erosion of expertise until the company can no longer perform the role the country once depended upon.
Petronas remains one of the strongest national oil companies globally and is far from that position.
However, institutions of this calibre are seldom damaged by a single decision. They are weakened incrementally through a series of demands that, viewed individually, may all appear reasonable.
GROWING PRESSURE ON MULTIPLE FRONTS
Petronas entered 2026 facing mounting challenges.
In 2025, before the Hormuz disruption, the company recorded a 17 per cent decline in revenue and an 18 per cent fall in profit due to lower oil prices and weaker sales.
The closure of the Strait of Hormuz then pushed crude prices up by almost 40 per cent, increasing shipping and insurance costs while Petronas continued ensuring domestic fuel supplies remained uninterrupted.
At the same time, Sarawak's legal challenge over federal petroleum laws has created additional uncertainty for long-term investment decisions.
Oil and gas developments typically require investment horizons of between 15 and 20 years.
Planning such projects becomes significantly more difficult when the legal framework governing future returns remains unresolved.
Each individual pressure may be understandable.
Taken together, however, they place considerable strain on a company already operating in an increasingly complex environment.
A NATIONAL ASSET BUILT OVER GENERATIONS
Countries that possess globally competitive companies rarely take them for granted.
South Korea protects Samsung, while Taiwan safeguards TSMC — not simply out of national pride, but because rebuilding organisations of that scale and capability would take decades, if it were possible at all.
Malaysia built Petronas under circumstances that no longer exist, benefiting from the political certainty of the 1970s, a unique period in the global energy industry and a long-term national vision.
Questions over how Petronas' revenue should be shared will continue, and they deserve public discussion.
However, priorities matter.
Tax arrangements and royalty formulas can be renegotiated. Court proceedings will eventually conclude.
The institutional knowledge, technical capability and global expertise that enabled Malaysia to weather the Strait of Hormuz disruption cannot simply be recreated if they are allowed to erode.
The Hormuz crisis will eventually pass.
The long-term cost of getting Petronas wrong may not.
SYED FARHAN SYED FEIZAL
Energy and Geopolitical Analyst,
Global Asia Consulting
* The views expressed in this article are the author's own and do not necessarily reflect those of the New Straits Times.
